mnemonics.title: Frame Buffer Purpose
mnemonics.content: FRAME = For Rendering And Memory - Stores pixel data for display refresh cycles continuously

acronyms.usage: Color model with transparency; each pixel stores 4 color components
Specialized memory for graphics operations with dual-port access
Stores depth values for hidden surface removal in 3D graphics
Palette mapping pixel indices to actual colors, used in limited color modes

acronyms.expansion: Color Lookup COde for display - Memory holding color palette values

visualTips.visualization: Think of frame buffer as a 2D grid matching screen resolution. Each cell (pixel) has a memory location with color value stored sequentially row by row.
8-bit = 256 colors (small palette), 16-bit = 65K colors (65,536), 24-bit = 16.7 million colors, 32-bit = 16.7M + transparency. More bits = smoother color gradients.
Imagine two canvases: one shown to audience while artist paints on second; when finished, swap them instantly - viewer never sees the painting process.

analogies.analogy: Like a digital canvas where each pixel is a colored dot - the complete painting is stored before being shown on screen.
Like layers in paper cutout animation - Z-buffer remembers the depth order so front objects hide back objects.
Like flipping through a flipbook - faster flipping (higher Hz) makes animation appear smoother.
Like number of available paint colors - more colors (higher bits) = more realistic and smooth gradients.
